A0:
# Memo: Build vs. Buy — Internal Analytics Dashboard

**To:** Engineering & Data Leadership
**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Status:** Draft for decision

---

## 1. Decision

Whether to **build** our internal analytics dashboard in-house or **buy** an off-the-shelf product (e.g., a BI/embedded-analytics tool) and configure it.

> **Recommendation up front:** **Buy first, build later.** Adopt an off-the-shelf tool now to get reporting in users' hands fast, and reserve in-house build for the *specific* views/metrics that become a competitive or workflow differentiator. Revisit in ~6 months once usage patterns are clear.

*(This memo states assumptions explicitly — adjust the recommendation if any are wrong.)*

## 2. Context & assumptions

- **Audience:** internal teams (ops, product, leadership), not external customers. → Customization and polish matter less than coverage and speed.
- **Data already lives** in a warehouse/DB we control.
- **No dedicated analytics-platform team** exists today; building means pulling from product engineering.
- Need is **"good enough" decision-support reporting**, not a real-time, high-scale, or customer-facing product.

If the dashboard is **customer-facing / a revenue product**, the calculus flips toward build — flag this and re-scope.

## 3. Options

| | **Buy** (BI/analytics SaaS) | **Build** (in-house) |
|---|---|---|
| Time to first value | Days–weeks | Months |
| Upfront cost | Low (subscription) | High (eng time) |
| Ongoing cost | Per-seat/usage fees (grows with users) | Maintenance + on-call (hidden, persistent) |
| Customization | Limited to tool's model | Unlimited |
| Data control / security | Depends on vendor & deployment | Full control |
| Opportunity cost | Eng stays on core product | Diverts eng from core product |
| Lock-in risk | Moderate–high | Low (but you own all bugs) |

## 4. Decision criteria (weighted)

1. **Time-to-value** — how fast do stakeholders need numbers? *(High weight)*
2. **Total cost of ownership over 2–3 yrs** — include maintenance, not just license. *(High)*
3. **Differentiation** — is *how* we analyze a competitive edge, or table stakes? *(High)*
4. **Data sensitivity / compliance** — can data leave our perimeter? *(Medium–High)*
5. **Customization depth** — do standard charts/filters cover 80% of needs? *(Medium)*

**Rule of thumb:** Build only where the work is *core, differentiating, and not well-served by vendors.* Internal reporting is rarely all three.

## 5. Analysis

- **Buy wins on speed and focus.** The fastest path to decisions-from-data is configuring an existing tool. Most internal needs (funnels, trends, cohort tables, exec summaries) are exactly what BI tools are built for.
- **Build's real cost is the long tail.** A dashboard is never "done" — auth, access control, new metrics, schema changes, and breakages become a permanent tax on a team that has no mandate to own it.
- **Hybrid is the pragmatic default.** Buy for breadth; build thin, owned components only for the few metrics/workflows that are genuinely unique to us and where a vendor can't express the logic.

## 6. Recommendation & next steps

**Adopt "Buy first, build selectively."**

1. **Shortlist 2–3 tools** against the criteria above; weight time-to-value and TCO. *(Owner: Data lead — 1 wk)*
2. **Run a 2-week pilot** on one real use case with 3–5 actual users; measure setup effort + whether it answers the questions. *(2 wks)*
3. **List "build-only" gaps** the pilot reveals — metrics/views no tool handles. *(during pilot)*
4. **Decide:** if the tool covers ≥80% of needs → buy + small custom layer. If it covers <50% → reconsider a scoped build with a named owner and maintenance budget.

## 7. Open questions (resolve before finalizing)

- Is this strictly internal, or will it ever be customer-facing?
- Any compliance constraint that prevents data leaving our infrastructure?
- Is there a budget ceiling (subscription) vs. headcount availability (build)?

---

Want me to tailor this to specifics (expected user count, budget, candidate tools, data stack), turn it into a one-page exec version, or save it as a file?
A1s:
Here's a decision memo you can drop into a doc or send as-is. It takes a position (buy/adopt for the standard layer, build only the thin differentiated slice) and flags the conditions that would flip it. I've marked the few assumptions I made so you can correct them.

# Decision Memo: Build vs. Buy — Internal Analytics Dashboard

**To:** Eng / Data leadership
**Date:** 2026-06-23
**Decision owner:** _[fill in]_
**Status:** Recommendation, pending input on the assumptions in §5

## Recommendation

**Buy (adopt an existing BI tool) for the standard dashboard layer; build only the thin slice that is genuinely specific to us.** An internal analytics dashboard is almost never a competitive differentiator, so engineering time spent rebuilding charting, auth, scheduling, and data connectors is time not spent on the product. Start on an off-the-shelf tool now; revisit a custom build only if we hit a concrete wall (see §4).

## Context

We need internal visibility into _[product/revenue/funnel — fill in]_ metrics. The question is whether to build a dashboard in-house or adopt a third-party tool. This memo assumes the audience is internal (our own team), not a customer-facing analytics feature — that distinction drives the recommendation.

## The tradeoff

| | Build | Buy / Adopt |
|---|---|---|
| **Time to first value** | Weeks to months | Days |
| **Eng cost** | Ongoing — owns charting, auth, alerts, connectors, maintenance | Low — config and modeling only |
| **Fit / control** | Exact, full control | High for common needs; some constraints |
| **Total cost** | Salary-heavy, grows with scope | License/seat fee, predictable |
| **Risk** | Reinventing solved problems; bus-factor on a side system | Vendor lock-in, data egress, pricing changes |

The asymmetry that matters: building reliably costs *more than the visible feature* — you also inherit access control, scheduled refresh, embeddings/sharing, mobile rendering, and years of maintenance on a system that doesn't differentiate us.

## When this recommendation flips to "build"

Lean toward building only if **two or more** of these hold:
- The dashboard *is* the product, or a customer-facing feature we'd sell.
- Our data model or security requirements are so specific that no tool models them without heavy contortion.
- Data residency/compliance forbids sending data to a third-party SaaS, and self-hosting a vendor tool is also off the table.
- We've already adopted a tool and hit a hard, named limitation (not a hypothetical).

## Recommended path

1. **Adopt a BI tool for the 80%.** Candidates: an open-source self-hostable tool (e.g. Metabase or Superset) if data residency or cost-at-scale matters; a managed tool (e.g. Looker/Power BI/Mode-class) if we'd rather not run infrastructure.
2. **Invest in the data layer, not the dashboard.** The durable asset is clean, well-modeled tables and clear metric definitions. That work transfers to any tool and is where build effort actually pays off.
3. **Build only the thin custom slice** — a specific internal view a generic tool can't render — and only after the off-the-shelf tool proves insufficient for it.
4. **Set a 30-day checkpoint:** does the adopted tool cover ≥80% of asks? If yes, done. If no, the gaps become a scoped build decision with real evidence behind it.

## Open inputs needed (assumptions to confirm)

- **Audience:** internal-only? (Assumed yes — flips the call if this is customer-facing.)
- **Data residency / compliance** constraints that would rule out SaaS?
- **Budget shape:** is recurring license spend or one-time eng cost easier to absorb?
- **Existing stack:** are we already paying for a tool (Power BI via O365, Looker via GCP, etc.) we should default to?

---

If you give me the four inputs in §5 — especially audience and data-residency — I'll tighten this into a final version with a specific tool recommendation and a rough cost line instead of the generic framing.

If yes, done. If no, the gaps become a scoped build decision with real evidence behind it.

## Open inputs needed (assumptions to confirm)

- **Audience:** internal-only? (Assumed yes — flips the call if this is customer-facing.)
- **Coverage target & checkpoint:** is the ~80%-coverage bar and 30-day review window in §4 right for us, or should both be reset?
- **Data residency / compliance** constraints that would rule out SaaS?
- **Budget shape:** is recurring license spend or one-time eng cost easier to absorb?
